perturbation.original_text: Party A shall charge Party B energy saving service fee according to the income from CDQ waste heat power generation station.
perturbation.changed_text: Party A shall charge Party B energy saving service fee, which shall be considered a tax, according to the income from CDQ waste heat power generation station.
perturbation.explanation: The contract specifies that Party A charges Party B an 'energy saving service fee.' Defining this fee as a 'tax' creates a legal contradiction, because only governmental entities can impose taxes. A private company cannot legally impose a tax.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The power to tax is generally reserved for governmental entities, not private companies.
perturbation.law_citation: U.S. Constitution, Article I, Section 8, Clause 1
perturbation.law_explanation: The U.S. Constitution grants Congress the power to lay and collect taxes. This power is generally understood to be exclusive to governmental entities. A private contract attempting to define a fee as a tax would be unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article I | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article I Article I describes the design of the legislative branch of US Government -- the Congress. Important ideas include the separation of powers between branches of government (checks and balances), the election of Senators and Representatives, the process by which laws are made, and the powers that Congress has. Learn more... Primary tabs Section 1. All legislative powers herein granted shall be vested in a Congress of the United States, which shall consist of a Senate and House of Representatives. Section 2. The House of Representatives shall be composed of members chosen every second year by the people of the several states, and the electors in each state shall have the qualifications requisite for electors of the most numerous branch of the state legislature. No person shall be a Representative who shall not have attained to the age of twenty five years, and been seven years a citizen of the United States, and who shall not, when elected, be an inhabitant of that state in which he shall be chosen. Representatives and direct taxes shall be apportioned among the several states which may be included within this union, according to their respective numbers, which shall be determined by adding to the whole number of free persons, including those bound to service for a term of years, and excluding Indians not taxed, three fifths of all other Persons. The actual Enumeration shall be made within three years after the first meeting of the Congress of the United States, and within every subsequent term of ten years, in such manner as they shall by law direct. The number of Representatives shall not exceed one for every thirty thousand, but each state shall have at least one Representative; and until such enumeration shall be made, the state of New Hampshire shall be entitled to chuse three, Massachusetts eight, Rhode Island and Providence Plantations one, Connecticut five, New York six, New Jersey four, Pennsylvania eight, Delaware one, Maryland six, Virginia ten, North Carolina five, South Carolina five, and Georgia three. When vacancies happen in the Representation from any state, the executive authority thereof shall issue writs of election to fill such vacancies. The House of Representatives shall choose their speaker and other officers; and shall have the sole power of impeachment. Section 3. The Senate of the
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines an 'energy saving service fee' as a 'tax'. The law explanation states that the power to tax is generally understood to be exclusive to governmental entities. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Article I of the U.S. Constitution, supports this by stating that 'All legislative powers herein granted shall be vested in a Congress of the United States'. A private company defining a fee as a tax directly contradicts this constitutional allocation of power, as a private company cannot legally impose a tax. This creates a clear non-compliance, making the contradiction direct and serious.

perturbation.original_text: After the termination of the contact, Party B has the disposition right to the project.
perturbation.changed_text: After the termination of the contact, Party B has the disposition right to the project, which includes the ability to sell the intellectual property of Party A.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract states Party B has disposition rights after termination, but clarifies in item Eight that 'the intellectual property of the project belongs to Party A.' Adding that the disposition right includes selling Party A's IP directly contradicts the prior statement and IP law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Intellectual property rights are protected by law, and ownership dictates who can sell or transfer those rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: Copyright law (17 U.S.C. § 201) vests ownership of copyright in the author of the work. Modifying the contract to allow Party B to sell Party A's intellectual property after termination directly contradicts this established principle of intellectual property law, rendering that clause likely unenforceable regarding the intellectual property.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 17 / CHAPTER 2 / § 201 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 17 USC 201 : Ownership of copyright Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 17-COPYRIGHTS CHAPTER 2-COPYRIGHT OWNERSHIP AND TRANSFER Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §201. Ownership of copyright (a) Initial Ownership .-Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire .-In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by th
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts copyright law. The original contract gives Party B 'disposition right to the project' after termination. The changed version expands this to include 'the ability to sell the intellectual property of Party A'. According to 17 U.S.C. § 201, 'Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work'. Since Party A owns the intellectual property, granting Party B the right to sell it after termination directly violates Party A's copyright ownership, thus creating a legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2 reinforces that copyright initially vests in the author.

perturbation.original_text: If the project cannot be completed on schedule or supply power normally due to force majeure, such as war, flood, and earthquake, both parties shall be partially or fully exempt from their liabilities based on the effects of force majeure.
perturbation.changed_text: If the project cannot be completed on schedule or supply power normally due to force majeure, such as war, flood, and earthquake, both parties shall be exempt from all liabilities regardless of the effects of force majeure.
perturbation.explanation: The original text correctly states that liabilities are excused 'based on the effects of force majeure'. The changed text removes this qualification, stating that *all* liabilities are excused regardless of the actual impact. This could lead to a situation where a minor force majeure event excuses a major breach, which is not legally sound.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The scope of excusable delay or non-performance under force majeure clauses is typically limited to the extent that the force majeure event directly causes the delay or non-performance.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-615 addresses excuse by failure of presupposed conditions. While it allows for excused performance due to unforeseen circumstances, this is generally interpreted to mean that the excuse is proportional to the impact of the event. The modified contract language, stating 'regardless of the effects', contradicts this principle by potentially excusing significant breaches for minor events.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. Except so far as a seller may have assumed a greater obligation and subject to the preceding section on substituted performance: (a) Delay in delivery or non-delivery in whole or in part by a seller who complies with paragraphs (b) and (c) is not a breach of his duty under a contract for sale if performance as agreed has been made impracticable by the occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made or by compliance in good faith with any applicable foreign or domestic governmental regulation or order whether or not it later proves to be invalid. (b) Where the causes mentioned in paragraph (a) affect only a part of the seller's capacity to perform, he must allocate production and deliveries among his customers but may at his option include regular customers not then under contract as well as his own requirements for further manufacture. He may so allocate in any manner which is fair and reasonable. (c) The seller must notify the buyer seasonably that there will be delay or non-delivery and, when allocation is required under paragraph (b), of the estimated quota thus made available for the buyer. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts UCC § 2-615, as interpreted in the Law Explanation. The original text limited liability exemptions based on the effects of force majeure, aligning with the principle that excuses should be proportional to the event's impact. The modified text eliminates this proportionality, excusing 'all liabilities regardless of the effects,' which clashes with the idea that performance is excused only when 'performance as agreed has been made impracticable,' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The change removes a crucial qualification, potentially excusing significant breaches for minor events, which is legally unsound.
